questions.prompt: The walkthrough completes the Low in about 11 minutes. What does it call the defining quality of that timing?
choices: Unremarkable. The six-step workflow executed cleanly with no second-guessing, no acting on context, no drift at close.

choices.label: Fast — the tech moved quickly by skipping verify.
choices.rationale: The 11 minutes includes the post-remediation wait and verify; skipping verify is the rhythm-breaker.

choices.label: Slow — most Lows should close in under 5 minutes.
choices.rationale: 10 minutes per Low is the right cadence. Faster usually means a skipped step.

choices.label: Exceptional — the tech investigated thoroughly before approving.
choices.rationale: Investigation is the second-guessing failure.

questions.explanation: Unremarkable is the point. Routine Lows should feel like one motion, not eight discrete checks.

questions.prompt: The autoruns entry points to a PUP at C:\Users\Public\Updater\rupdate.exe. The Recommendation removes the autoruns key but NOT the file. Why didn't the SOC recommend removing the file?
choices: The SOC's classification of the binary as a PUP drove a persistence-only remediation. Without the autoruns key, it doesn't run on its own.

choices.label: The SOC forgot to include the file remediation; you should add it yourself.
choices.rationale: Acting on context. Absence in the Recommendation is a decision, not an oversight.

choices.label: Huntress only supports one remediation per incident.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts.

choices.label: Files can't be removed remotely by Huntress agents.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. File remediation is a standard type.

questions.explanation: The file is in Evidence; without the persistence mechanism it's inert. The SOC weighed and decided.

questions.prompt: "Let me sandbox this binary just to be sure before approving." What does the walkthrough say about this on a Low?
choices: Investigating the binary because the path looks suspicious is the second-guessing failure. The SOC has done the analysis; the path looking suspicious is exactly what the SOC saw and classified. Adds 30 minutes to a 10-minute ticket.

choices.label: Always sandbox on a Low; it's the safest practice.
choices.rationale: Sandboxing is investigation work above the helpdesk ceiling.

choices.label: Sandbox only if the file is in C:\Users\Public\.
choices.rationale: The path doesn't license the investigation. The SOC's classification already weighed it.

choices.label: Sandbox is required for any PUP classification.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson. PUP classifications are within the SOC's remit.

questions.explanation: Investigation above the helpdesk ceiling. The SOC has done it.

questions.prompt: "Let me remove the file as well as the autoruns entry for completeness." What's the failure?
choices: Acting on context. The Recommendation specified autoruns only; the file is in Evidence but not in the action zone.

choices.label: No failure — "completeness" is the lesson's recommended posture.
choices.rationale: It's the named rhythm-breaker. Completeness isn't a virtue here.

choices.label: The tech should remove the file but NOT the autoruns entry.
choices.rationale: Inverts what the Recommendation asks; "softening" the recommendation.

choices.label: The file should be quarantined to a network share before deletion.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson and not part of the Huntress remediation flow.

questions.explanation: Don't go further than the SOC asked. Completeness here is just acting on context.

questions.prompt: A peer closes their Low in 8 minutes instead of 11 because "the portal said applied." What's the failure?
choices: Skipping the post-remediation wait. Most of the time it's fine; the times it isn't are the re-detection-within-minutes cases that catch you out three days later when the same incident re-fires.

choices.label: 8 minutes is too fast; the tech should slow down.
choices.rationale: Speed in isolation isn't the failure; honouring the wait+verify is.

choices.label: The PSA close was skipped, not the wait.
choices.rationale: The walkthrough names the wait as the named skip.

choices.label: No failure — "applied" is sufficient confirmation.
choices.rationale: "Applied" and the verify check are different.

questions.explanation: Skipping the wait is what creates the re-detection that lands on the next shift's queue with no context.

questions.prompt: The fourth incident in your shift's Low queue is the same PUP autoruns pattern as the first, on a different customer. What's the right reflex on the current incident?

choices.label: Recognise the pattern and stop to dig deeper before processing.
choices.rationale: Pattern observation is worth surfacing, but the processing of this Low isn't paused. The SOC has classified.

choices: Run the walkthrough exactly as on the first incident — claim, review, approve, verify, close, document.

choices.label: Skip the verify step because "same pattern, same result."
choices.rationale: Skipping verify is the rhythm-breaker.

questions.explanation: No reason to deviate. The pattern goes in the EOD note; the current Low gets processed normally.

questions.prompt: At end of shift, you've processed five Low incidents with the same PUP pattern across three customers. What do you do?

choices.label: Nothing extra; each was processed correctly.
choices.rationale: The pattern is worth surfacing. Might be a campaign, a bundled-software wave, or background noise.

choices: Add a one-line entry to your EOD handover note surfacing the pattern at the portfolio level.

choices.label: Reply to the SOC analyst on each individual incident with "are you seeing this elsewhere?"
choices.rationale: Interrupts the SOC's per-incident workflow with a portfolio question.

questions.explanation: You did the work, observed the pattern, handed it up. The senior or SOC does portfolio-level analysis.

questions.prompt: The walkthrough involved no customer call. Why?
choices: A Low with no user verification asked for in the Recommendation is a Low that doesn't need a customer call. The Recommendation is the contract.

choices.label: Customer calls are always skipped for Lows.
choices.rationale: Too absolute. If the Recommendation calls for verification, the call happens.

choices.label: The tech forgot to call; that was a mistake in the walkthrough.
choices.rationale: The walkthrough is the model. The no-call is deliberate.

choices.label: The customer had opted out of all comms in their account settings.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson.

questions.explanation: Don't manufacture customer calls to "be thorough." The Recommendation drives the call.

questions.prompt: The file stayed on the endpoint (the SOC didn't recommend file removal). A week later the same PUP autoruns is re-detected on the same endpoint. What does the lesson say?
choices: The file was still there; the SOC's recommendation was deliberate (persistence, not file). A pattern of re-detection might warrant the SOC's reconsideration — that's a clarifying reply, not a unilateral move.

choices.label: The tech should have removed the file the first time "for completeness."
choices.rationale: Acting on context. The SOC's call was deliberate.

choices.label: Run the remediation again and remove the file this time without asking.
choices.rationale: Unilateral expansion on a re-detection.

choices.label: Close the new incident as a duplicate of the previous one.
choices.rationale: The new incident is a new event.

questions.explanation: A re-detection pattern is the right surface for a clarifying reply to the SOC, not for unilateral expansion.

questions.prompt: The walkthrough avoided the drift trap because the tech closed Huntress and the PSA in sequence (Huntress at 11:52, PSA at 11:53) with the note already in the PSA.
questions.explanation: Both systems closed in sequence, no gap. That's how the drift trap stays a trap that doesn't catch you.
